title: Entanglement Pattern Transition of Quantum States from Directed Percolation
authors: Julian Boesl, Frank Pollmann, Michael Knap
abstract: Changes in the entanglement structure and critical phenomena are hallmarks of quantum phase transitions. Here, we discuss how they appear in transitions between classes of states with distinct entanglement patterns beyond the paradigm of stable equilibrium phases of matter. Using a mapping between stochastic automata and isometric Tensor Network States (isoTNS), we construct a two-dimensional quantum state from the Domany-Kinzel automaton, which is a (1+1)D process with an absorbing phase transition in the directed percolation class. At the critical point of the automaton, the corresponding isoTNS hosts algebraic correlations in all spatial directions. The continuous parent Hamiltonian of this state has a degenerate ground state manifold. It consists of a product state (the absorbing state) and a second state that undergoes a transition from pairwise entanglement between distant regions, similarly to the W state, to a state with trivial entanglement. Our results demonstrate how the correspondence between isoTNS and classical stochastic evolution can be used to probe the Hilbert space structure beyond stable ground state manifolds.
summary: The paper establishes a connection between classical directed percolation and quantum entanglement transitions. It shows that the absorbing phase transition of a stochastic automaton manifests as a transition between W-state-like entanglement and trivial entanglement in a corresponding tensor network state.
main_problem: Investigating transitions between quantum states with distinct entanglement patterns that do not represent stable equilibrium phases of matter, specifically by mapping classical non-equilibrium phase transitions to quantum tensor networks.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that the directed percolation (DP) transition in a classical automaton corresponds to a quantum entanglement transition between a W-state-like pattern (long-range pairwise entanglement) and a trivial, short-range entangled pattern.
method: The study uses an 'automaton-to-isoTNS' mapping to translate stochastic cellular automata (Domany-Kinulence) into isometric Tensor Network States, utilizing transfer matrix analysis and negativity to quantify entanglement.
model_or_system: A 2D quantum state constructed from the (1+1)D Domany-Kinzel automaton, featuring a continuous, frustration-free parent Hamiltonian with a degenerate ground state manifold consisting of a vacuum state and a non-trivial state.
key_observables: Negativity (as a measure of bipartite entanglement), Z-operator expectation values, density of active sites, and normalized correlation functions.
important_parameters: Transition probabilities (p1, p2, p3) of the DK automaton, specifically the p1=0 plane, and the critical point p_c approx 0.7055.
main_assumptions: The tensor network is not injective, meaning the constructed state might not be the unique ground state of the parent Hamiltonian; the derivation of scaling laws assumes translational invariance.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the mapping from the DK automaton to the quantum state and the phase diagram; Figure 4 illustrates the structure of the parent Hamiltonian and the projection of qubits.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the mapping between stochastic automata and isoTNS, analyzes the entanglement transition in the absorbing phase of the DK model, compares the results to the Kasteleyn model to highlight the role of symmetry, and extends the findings to 3D directed percolation.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant for many-body dynamics and open quantum systems as it provides a blueprint for using classical non-equilibrium universality classes to discover and characterize new, non-trivial entanglement structures in quantum states.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. The Domany-Kinzel (DK) automaton. a) In the DK au- tomaton, even (i) and odd ( j and k) sites are updated alternatingly. White/gray circles represent empty/active sites (0/1); the probabili- ties of an empty site at i are P(0| jk) = 1−P(1| jk). By adding physical legs, the automaton is mapped to a quantum state; time t becomes a spatial direction y. b) The phase diagram of the DK automaton for p1 = 0. At low p2/3, the automaton is in the absorbing phase; the or- der parameter ni = (1−⟨Zi⟩)/2 is zero. In the active phase at high p2/3, ni is finite. The transition line (black) is taken from [43]. For peri- odic boundaries, the ground state manifold of the parent Hamiltonian is...

FIG. 2. Normalized correlations. The normalized correlations Ci norm( j) in the directions y (red), x (blue) and x −y (purple), eval- uated in an open boundary system with fully active boundaries for p2 = p3 = p. a) Correlations on the critical line p = 0.7055. All directions feature algebraic scaling with exponent β/ν∥in y direction and β/ν⊥in all other directions. b) Correlations in the absorbing phase, p = 0.55 decay exponentially. c) Correlations in the active phase, p = 0.8 saturate to a finite value, corresponding to the den- sity n of active sites in the steady state. The linear system size is L = 3000 in a) and c), and L = 100 in b).

FIG. 4. Parent Hamiltonian HDK. Top: The support of the projector B∨. The outer qubits j1, . . . j6 are acted on only diagonally in the Z basis, while the inner qubits i1, i2 are projected onto a superposition α |00⟩+ β |11⟩; the coefficients depend on the environment. Bottom: The structure of the Hamiltonian HDK in the DP plane p1 = 0. The Hilbert space splits up into sectors labeled by the defect number ND. The ground state manifold lies in the sector ND = 0. As the state |vac⟩= |00 · · · 0⟩cannot be connected to any other zero-defect state, it is as an additional ground state along the non-trivial state |GS⟩.
